title: Git vs. GitHub, and What a Commit Is
choices: A saved snapshot of your changes, with a message describing what changed
A live, always-on connection to a remote server
A backup copy of your entire computer
A chat message sent to your teammates

content.explanation: Git is version control software that tracks changes to files over time, running entirely on your own machine. GitHub is a website that hosts Git repositories online so teams can share and collaborate on them — Git is the tool, GitHub is one place to store and share what it tracks (GitLab and Bitbucket are alternatives). Every time you save a meaningful set of changes in Git, you create a 'commit': a snapshot of exactly what the files looked like at that moment, with a short message explaining why.
content.example:
git add ntp_check.py
git commit -m "Add NTP drift check task"
git push origin feature/ntp-check
# 'commit' saves a snapshot locally; 'push' uploads that snapshot to GitHub

acceptable_answers: a saved snapshot of your changes, with a message describing what changed
solution.explanation: A commit is a snapshot of your files at a point in time, paired with a message describing the change — this is what lets you see history, compare versions, and revert if something breaks.
